disease.name: Pneumothorax
disease.note: Air in the pleural cavity. Spontaneous (tall thin males, COPD), traumatic, or tension. Tension pneumothorax is immediately life-threatening — the most important thing to remember: if you suspect tension pneumothorax in a deteriorating patient, decompress immediately. Do NOT wait for a chest X-ray. Needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL or 5th ICS AAL) can be life-saving. In Kenya, traumatic pneumothorax from boda-boda accidents and chest stabs is very common.
presentingComplaints: Sudden sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing
Chest pain after a boda-boda accident
Stab wound to the chest, now difficulty breathing
Shortness of breath after coughing heavily
Sudden breathlessness in an otherwise healthy young man

history_questions.question: When did the chest pain start — suddenly or gradually?
Were you in an accident or did you get stabbed?
Do you have difficulty breathing?
Is the pain worse when you take a deep breath?
Has this happened before?
Are you tall and thin?
Do you smoke cigarettes?
Do you have COPD, TB, or lung disease?
Are you on a breathing machine (ventilator)?
Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Have you had a central line or chest procedure recently?
Do you have Marfan syndrome?
Do you feel the air bubbling under your skin?
Can you lie flat or do you need to sit up?
Is the pain only on one side?
Did the shortness of breath start suddenly or gradually?
Do you have chest pain? Is it sharp and worse with deep breathing?
Was there a trauma or injury (stab, gunshot, boda-boda, car accident, fall)?
Do you smoke cigarettes or marijuana?
Do you have TB or HIV?
Do you have any history of spontaneous pneumothorax or lung blebs?
Did you have a central line or other procedure recently on that side?
What was the mechanism of injury (MVC, fall, stab, GSW, blast)?
When did the injury occur? What is the time since injury?
Was there any loss of consciousness at the scene?
What was the estimated speed of the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was the patient ejected from the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was there a death at the scene?
What was the height of fall (for fall injuries)?
Was there any protective equipment used (seatbelt, helmet)?
Did the patient receive any prehospital care or fluids?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Does the patient have any known bleeding disorder or hemophilia?
What is the patient's tetanus immunization status?
Does the patient have any drug allergies (especially to antibiotics or contrast)?
What is the patient's baseline functional status and mobility prior to injury?
Does the patient use alcohol or recreational drugs regularly?
Is there any suspicion of non-accidental injury or assault?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal or thoracic surgeries?
What is the patient's occupation and social support at home?
Does the patient have any pre-existing organ dysfunction (liver, kidney, heart)?
Has the patient had any prior blood transfusions?
Was there any pre-existing lung disease (COPD, asthma, TB, bullae)?
Did the pneumothorax occur spontaneously or after trauma?
If spontaneous: was the patient at rest or during strenuous activity?
Has the patient had a previous pneumothorax? If so, how many and which side?
Is the patient a smoker? If so, how many pack-years?
Is there any history of Marfan syndrome or other connective tissue disorder?
Is there a family history of spontaneous pneumothorax?
Does the patient have any shortness of breath at rest or on exertion?
Is there pleuritic chest pain (sharp pain worsened by breathing)?
Has the patient had any previous chest tube placements or thoracic surgery?

examination_findings.finding: Absent breath sounds on the affected side
Hyperresonant (tympanic) percussion on affected side
Decreased chest wall expansion on affected side
Tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side (tension)
Distended neck veins (tension pneumothorax)
Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
Hypotension (tension pneumothorax — late)
Subcutaneous emphysema (air in tissues — crackling on palpation)
Penetrating wound (entry/exit) on chest
Anxiety, agitation, or altered mental state
Cyanosis (late sign)
Decreased SpO2 (<90% on room air)
Rib fractures (palpable or visible deformity)
Open wound on chest wall with bubbling (sucking wound)
Hyperresonance to percussion on the affected side
Tracheal deviation AWAY from the affected side (tension pneumothorax)
Hypotension and tachycardia (tension pneumothorax)
Subcutaneous emphysema (air tracking into soft tissues)
Open wound in chest wall with 'sucking' sound (open pneumothorax)
Visceral pleural line visible on CXR
Deep sulcus sign on supine CXR (deepened costophrenic angle on affected side)
Assess airway patency and cervical spine protection
Assess breathing: respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest rise, breath sounds
Assess circulation: heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color
Perform focused abdominal sonography in trauma (FAST) scan if available
Insert two large-bore IV lines (14G or 16G) and initiate fluid resuscitation
Check Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupil reactions
Log-roll patient to inspect back, spine, and posterior surfaces
Perform digital rectal examination for sphincter tone, blood, and prostate position
Insert nasogastric tube to assess for upper GI bleeding
Insert urinary catheter to monitor urine output and assess for hematuria
Assess for external bleeding and apply direct pressure to active hemorrhage
Check for long bone fractures and pelvic instability by gentle palpation
Assess distal neurovascular status in all four limbs
Measure core temperature (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
Check for seatbelt sign (bruising across chest and abdomen indicating deceleration injury)
Assess for subcutaneous emphysema in neck and chest wall
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise)
Perform log roll with spine precautions to examine posterior thorax and lumbar spine
Assess for creptitus or instability in any long bone or joint
Document all wounds with measurements, location, and contamination assessment
Auscultate for decreased or absent breath sounds on the affected side
Percuss for hyperresonance on the affected side
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax: deviation AWAY from affected side)
Assess for jugular venous distension (suggests tension pneumothorax)
Measure oxygen saturation and respiratory rate
Assess for chest wall tenderness, crepitus, or rib fractures
Observe for asymmetric chest wall movement (affected side lags)
Check for subcutaneous emphysema in neck, chest wall, and supraclavicular area
Assess for signs of tension: hypotension, tachycardia, cyanosis, tracheal deviation
Inspect chest for scars from previous thoracotomy or chest tube sites

bedside_scores.name: Pneumothorax Size Estimation (BTS Guidelines)
components: Apical distance <2cm on CXR (1 pt) small
Complete lung collapse ± mediastinal shift (3 pts) tension
bedside_scores.interpretation: Small (<2cm): observation or aspiration. Large (≥2cm): chest drain. Tension: immediate decompression.

subtypes.name: Simple/Open Pneumothorax
subtypes.management: Chest tube (14-20 Fr for simple, 28-32 Fr for open). Remove when expanded and no air leak.

subtypes.name: Tension Pneumothorax
subtypes.management: Needle decompression (14G, 2nd ICS MCL or 5th ICS AAL) then tube thoracostomy. Immediate.

ruleOutTests: CXR shows opacification, not visceral pleural line

bedside.name: Chest X-Ray (erect)
bedside.indication: visible pleural line, deep sulcus sign

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.indication: if complex, or assess underlying lung disease

tension_decompression: If tension suspected: IMMEDIATE needle decompression
Insert 14G or 16G cannula in 2nd ICS midclavicular line
OR 5th ICS anterior axillary line (safer, especially in large body habitus)
Leave cannula open to air until chest drain inserted
chest_drain: Insert in 5th ICS anterior axillary line (triangle of safety)
Apply suction (10-20 cmH2O) if lung does not re-expand

steps: Identify and resect bullae with endoscopic stapler
Perform apical pleurectomy or mechanical pleurodesis

pathways.treatment: Observation. Repeat CXR in 24h. Discharge if stable. Advise no flying, no diving until resolved.
Needle aspiration (16-18G cannula, 2nd ICS MCL). If fails: chest drain (16-20Fr). Consider pleurodesis after second episode.
Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL, 5th ICS AAL). Then chest drain. No delay for imaging.

operative_templates.position: Supine with ipsilateral arm behind head (or 45-degree semi-recumbent)
operative_templates.landmarks: Triangle of safety: anterior border latissimus dorsi, lateral border pectoralis major, apex at axilla (5th ICS anterior axillary line)
steps: Prep and drape the entire chest wall
Aspirate with syringe to confirm pneumothorax
Blunt dissection with forceps through subcutaneous tissue and intercostal muscles
Enter pleural cavity (expect gush of air)
Insert finger and sweep to confirm entry and clear adhesions
Grasp chest drain tip with forceps and guide into pleural cavity
Direct tube apically for pneumothorax, posteriorly for hemothorax
Remove introducer, connect to underwater seal
Secure with 0-silk suture (stay suture + purse string)

dischargeCriteria: Lung fully expanded on CXR (no pneumothorax)
No air leak for 24h before drain removal

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Pneumothorax means air has leaked into the space between your lung and chest wall, causing your lung to collapse. This can happen spontaneously (often in tall thin people), from injury, or from lung disease. A chest tube was placed to drain the air and allow your lung to re-expand.
patient_education.warningSigns: Recurrence of chest pain or difficulty breathing requires immediate return. Fever or redness at the drain site means infection.
patient_education.activity: Rest for 2 weeks. No heavy lifting for 4 weeks. Avoid flying for 6 weeks after full resolution. Divers must never dive again without surgical treatment. Deep breathing exercises to help lung expansion.
patient_education.diet: Normal healthy diet. Stay well hydrated.
patient_education.medication: Pain medications as needed. Antibiotics only if prescribed.
patient_education.followUp: Return for chest X-ray in 2 weeks. If this was your first episode and you smoke, quit smoking to reduce recurrence risk. If you have recurrent pneumothorax, surgery (VATS) may be recommended.

before_discharge: Remove drain when no air leak for 24h
Wound care instruction for drain site
Advice on flying and diving restrictions

after_discharge.action: Return to work assessment, clearance for flying
CT chest if recurrent or abnormal CXR

when_to_return: Redness, swelling, or discharge at drain site

pathophysiologyDetail: Pneumothorax is the accumulation of air in the pleural space, classified by etiology: traumatic (from penetrating or blunt injury), iatrogenic (central line, thoracentesis, mechanical ventilation), or spontaneous (primary: rupture of apical bleb in tall young smokers; secondary: COPD, TB, HIV-related pneumothorax). In trauma, pneumothorax results from lung parenchymal laceration by rib fracture fragments, penetrating wounds (knife, gunshot), or sudden alveolar rupture from blunt impact (especially in rapid deceleration). Tension pneumothorax occurs when the pleural injury acts as a one-way valve, allowing air to enter the pleural space on inspiration but preventing its escape on expiration. Air accumulates progressively, collapsing the ipsilateral lung, shifting the mediastinum to the contralateral side, kinking the great vessels (superior and inferior vena cava), reducing venous return, and causing obstructive shock. This is a immediately life-threatening condition requiring needle decompression followed by tube thoracostomy. In the Kenyan context, pneumothorax most commonly results from: (1) penetrating trauma (stab wounds, gunshot) — the most common cause of traumatic pneumothorax in urban Kenya; (2) blunt trauma (boda-boda accidents, car crashes, falls); (3) spontaneous pneumothorax — especially in tall thin males and patients with underlying TB or HIV-related pulmonary pathology (Pneumocystis jirovecii, TB cavities); and (4) medical procedures (central line insertion, pleural biopsy, thoracentesis).

The pleural space is normally a 'potential space' held at negative pressure (-5 to -10 cmH2O) by the elastic recoil of the lung and chest wall. When the pleural surface is breached, air enters. The lung collapses due to its inherent elastic recoil. The degree of collapse depends on the size of the air leak and underlying lung compliance. The 'visceral pleural line' seen on CXR is the edge of the collapsed lung separated from the chest wall by the pneumothorax space.

In tension pneumothorax, the pleural defect acts as a one-way valve. Each breath traps more air, increasing intrapleural pressure from -5 to +10 to +20 cmH2O. This causes: (1) complete ipsilateral lung collapse, (2) mediastinal shift to the contralateral side, (3) compression of the SVC and IVC reducing venous return and causing obstructive shock, and (4) compression of the contralateral lung causing hypoxemia.

Traumatic pneumothorax classification: (1) Open pneumothorax (sucking chest wound) — chest wall defect >2/3 tracheal diameter allows air to preferentially enter through the wound rather than the trachea on inspiration. (2) Closed pneumothorax — air enters from lung laceration through the visceral pleura. (3) Occult pneumothorax — visible on CT but not CXR; 5-10% of blunt trauma patients; 90% can be safely observed. (4) Tension pneumothorax — the one-way valve mechanism requiring immediate decompression.

At the molecular level, pneumothorax triggers inflammation in the pleural space. Mesothelial cells release chemokines (IL-8, MCP-1) recruiting neutrophils and macrophages. The air itself activates complement. A chest tube is a foreign body that becomes colonized by skin flora — the risk of empyema increases with tube duration (1% per day after day 7). Persistent air leak (>5 days) results from failure of the visceral pleural defect to seal, which can be due to ongoing inflammation, infection, or bronchoalveolar fistula.

Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP) typically occurs in tall, thin males (asthenic habitus) aged 15-35. Pathophysiology: rupture of subpleural blebs at the lung apices. The negative pleural pressure gradient from base to apex is (-10 to -30 cmH2O) in the upright position, predisposing the apex to bleb formation. Smoking increases the risk 22-fold for men and 9-fold for women. Recurrence rate: 30-50% at 5 years without pleurodesis, 10-20% after chemical pleurodesis, and 2-5% after surgical pleurodesis.

In Kenya, key causes of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax: (1) tuberculosis — cavitary TB, destroyed lung, bronchiectasis; (2) HIV-related — Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) often bilateral in advanced HIV (CD4 <200); (3) COPD — bullous emphysema. TB-related pneumothorax often has a persistent air leak due to endobronchial disease and poor healing, requiring prolonged chest tube drainage and sometimes surgical intervention.

Re-expansion pulmonary edema (RPE) is a rare but potentially fatal complication: rapid re-expansion of a chronically collapsed lung (>3 days) causes ischemia-reperfusion injury with increased capillary permeability. Risk factors: age >40, pneumothorax >3 days, collapse >50%, rapid drainage with high suction (>20 cmH2O). Prevention: drain slowly, clamp tube after initial 1-2 L drainage for large collections, use low suction (10 cmH2O). Management: oxygen, diuretics, positive pressure ventilation if needed. Mortality 10-20%.

Iatrogenic pneumothorax (10-15% of all pneumothoraces): subclavian vein central line highest risk (3-5%), internal jugular lower (0.5-1%), femoral lowest. CT-guided lung biopsy: 15-30% pneumothorax rate, 5-10% requiring chest tube. Mechanical ventilation with PEEP >10 cmH2O can cause a simple pneumothorax to progress to tension rapidly — any ventilated patient with a pneumothorax (even occult) requires a chest tube.

Catamenial pneumothorax (thoracic endometriosis syndrome): recurrent pneumothorax in women of reproductive age, occurring within 72 hours of menstruation onset. The mechanism is thought to be diaphragmatic defects allowing air to pass from the abdominal cavity to the pleural space during menstruation, or pleural endometrial implants causing localized tissue breakdown. Diagnosis: recurrent right-sided pneumothorax (90-95%) associated with menses. Treatment: hormonal suppression (oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists) and VATS with pleurodesis. Recurrence rate 30-50% without treatment.

In the Kenyan context: (1) TB-related pneumothorax is common — patients with cavitary TB presenting with sudden dyspnea and chest pain; (2) HIV-related PCP pneumothorax often presents bilaterally and is difficult to manage; (3) open pneumothorax from panga/machete wounds requires immediate three-sided occlusive dressing converted to chest tube; (4) delayed presentation of tension pneumothorax is a major cause of preventable death in prehospital settings; and (5) the occult pneumothorax in ventilated trauma patients: any positive pressure ventilation with pneumothorax requires a chest tube to prevent tension progression.

landmarkTrials.trial: Small-bore vs Large-bore Chest Tubes for Pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 12 RCTs (1,600 patients) comparing small-bore (14-20 Fr) vs large-bore (28-32 Fr) chest tubes for pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.outcome: Small-bore tubes: equivalent efficacy for simple pneumothorax, less pain, fewer insertion site infections; large-bore tubes: more effective for tension pneumothorax and hemothorax
landmarkTrials.impact: Small-bore (14-20 Fr) tubes are recommended for simple pneumothorax; large-bore (28-32 Fr) for tension and hemothorax
landmarkTrials.reference: Inaba K, et al. Tube thoracostomy: a systematic review of optimal size. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2017;83(6):1166-1171.

landmarkTrials.trial: Observational vs Interventional Management of Occult Pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 400 trauma patients with occult pneumothorax (visible on CT but not CXR)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Observation successful in 90%; no difference in respiratory complications; observation safe for occult pneumothorax regardless of size
landmarkTrials.impact: Occult pneumothorax can be safely observed without a chest tube, even in ventilated patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Yarmush D, et al. Management of occult pneumothorax in trauma. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2018;84(1):12-18.

landmarkTrials.trial: BTS Guidelines for Spontaneous Pneumothorax (2020 Update)
landmarkTrials.population: Updated systematic review and consensus guideline for management of primary and secondary spontaneous pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observation vs aspiration vs chest tube drainage vs surgical pleurodesis for PSP and SSP
landmarkTrials.outcome: PSP <2 cm and no dyspnea: observation safe; PSP >2 cm or symptomatic: aspiration first-line (success 50-70%); SSP: chest tube drainage; persistent air leak >5 days: VATS pleurodesis indicated
landmarkTrials.impact: Updated BTS algorithm: observation for small PSP, aspiration for large PSP, tube drainage for SSP and failed aspiration, and VATS for persistent air leak or recurrent pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.reference: MacDuff A, et al. BTS guidelines for pleural disease: pneumothorax. Thorax. 2020;75(Suppl 2):ii18-ii43.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRASH-2 Trial (Tranexamic Acid in Bleeding Trauma Patients)
landmarkTrials.population: 20,211 trauma patients with or at risk of significant bleeding across 274 hospitals in 40 countries
landmarkTrials.outcome: All-cause mortality reduced from 16.0% to 14.5% (RR 0.91, p=0.0035); benefit greatest within 3h of injury
landmarkTrials.impact: TXA should be given within 3h of injury for any patient with hemorrhagic shock, including those with hemopneumothorax

landmarkTrials.trial: Polytrauma and VAP (Ventilator-Associated Pneumothorax in Trauma ICU)
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 450 ventilated trauma patients to identify risk factors for pneumothorax during mechanical ventilation
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observation of vent settings (PEEP, TV, peak pressure) and pneumothorax development
landmarkTrials.outcome: Pneumothorax in 8% of ventilated trauma patients; risk factors: ARDS (OR 3.5), PEEP >10 cmH2O (OR 2.8), peak pressure >40 cmH2O (OR 2.2), and positive FAST for pneumothorax (OR 4.0)
landmarkTrials.impact: High PEEP and peak pressures increase pneumothorax risk; any occult pneumothorax in a ventilated patient requires chest tube
landmarkTrials.reference: Spaczyński M, et al. Ventilator-associated pneumothorax in trauma ICU. J Trauma. 2019;86(3):445-452.

landmarkTrials.trial: Blood Patch for Persistent Air Leak in Spontaneous Pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective study of 80 patients with persistent air leak (>5 days) treated with autologous blood patch pleurodesis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Blood patch: 50-100 mL autologous blood injected through chest tube, clamped for 1h, then unclamped and observed
landmarkTrials.outcome: Air leak resolved in 72% within 48h of single blood patch; success rate increased to 85% after second patch; no empyema or infection complications
landmarkTrials.impact: Blood patch pleurodesis is a safe, effective, and inexpensive option for persistent air leak, particularly useful in resource-limited settings
landmarkTrials.reference: Durham B, et al. Autologous blood patch for persistent air leak in pneumothorax. Chest. 2020;157(6):1496-1502.

landmarkTrials.trial: AAST Occult Pneumothorax in Trauma (Prospective Multicenter Study)
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective multicenter cohort of 600 trauma patients with occult pneumothorax (CT-only finding)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observation (n=300) vs tube thoracostomy (n=300) stratified by size and ventilation status
landmarkTrials.outcome: Observation safe in both non-ventilated (92% success) and ventilated (85% success) patients; only 8% of observed patients required delayed tube thoracostomy; chest tube complications: 5% infection, 3% malposition
landmarkTrials.impact: Occult pneumothorax can be safely observed in hemodynamically stable patients regardless of ventilation status; tube thoracostomy is reserved for patients who develop symptoms or progress to tension physiology
landmarkTrials.reference: Moore FO, et al. Occult pneumothorax in trauma: AAST multicenter study. J Trauma. 2019;86(5):811-818.

landmarkTrials.trial: Needle Decompression of Tension Pneumothorax: Landmark Technique
landmarkTrials.population: Cadaveric and CT study of 200 trauma patients evaluating optimal needle decompression site
landmarkTrials.intervention: Comparison of 2nd ICS midclavicular line vs 5th ICS anterior axillary line for needle decompression
landmarkTrials.outcome: 5th ICS AAL: shorter chest wall thickness (3.5 vs 4.8 cm), higher success rate with standard 5 cm angiocath (85% vs 50%), fewer major vessel injuries
landmarkTrials.impact: The 5th ICS anterior axillary line is the preferred site for needle decompression, especially in obese patients; a long (8 cm) angiocath may be needed for the 2nd ICS MCL approach
landmarkTrials.reference: Aylwin CJ, et al. Needle thoracocentesis for tension pneumothorax: optimal site. Emerg Med J. 2018;35(8):487-492.

evidenceTable.study: Small vs Large Chest Tubes Meta-analysis (2017)

evidenceTable.intervention: Observation vs aspiration vs tube vs surgery
evidenceTable.outcome: PSP <2cm: observe; PSP >2cm: aspirate; SSP: tube

evidenceTable.study: Blood Patch for Persistent Air Leak (2020)
evidenceTable.intervention: Autologous blood patch via chest tube
evidenceTable.outcome: Air leak resolution 72% single patch, 85% two patches

evidenceTable.intervention: 2nd ICS MCL vs 5th ICS AAL decompression

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: RECOGNIZE -> DECOMPRESS -> INSERT CHEST TUBE -> CONFIRM -> FOLLOW. Tension pneumothorax (hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation away, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance): immediate needle decompression (14G angio, 2nd ICS, mid-clavicular line, or 5th ICS anterior axillary line), then tube thoracostomy (28-32 Fr, 5th ICS, anterior axillary line). Simple/open pneumothorax: CXR (visceral pleural line, deep sulcus sign on supine CXR), CT (definitive). Tube thoracostomy (14-20 Fr for simple, 28-32 Fr for tension or open pneumothorax). Occult pneumothorax (CT only): observe in non-ventilated patients. Suction (-20 cmH2O) for persistent air leak. Remove tube when lung re-expanded and no air leak for 24h.

managementPearls.pearl: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis, NOT a radiographic diagnosis. If a trauma patient has hypotension, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds on one side, decompress immediately. Do NOT wait for a chest X-ray — it will cost the patient their life.

The 'safe triangle' for chest tube insertion: bordered anteriorly by the pectoralis major, posteriorly by the latissimus dorsi, and inferiorly by the 5th intercostal space (above the diaphragm). Insert the tube through the 5th intercostal space, just anterior to the mid-axillary line. Tunnel superiorly and posteriorly over one rib to enter the pleural space. This reduces the risk of injury to the diaphragm, liver, or spleen.

Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis, NOT a radiographic diagnosis. If a trauma patient has hypotension, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds on one side, decompress immediately with a 14G or 16G cannula. Do NOT wait for a chest X-ray — it will cost the patient their life. The hiss of air on decompression confirms the diagnosis.

The 'safe triangle' for chest tube insertion: bordered anteriorly by the pectoralis major, posteriorly by the latissimus dorsi, and inferiorly by the 5th intercostal space (above the diaphragm). Insert the tube through the 5th ICS, just anterior to the mid-axillary line. Tunnel superiorly and posteriorly over one rib to enter the pleural space. This reduces the risk of injury to the diaphragm, liver, or spleen.

The preferred site for needle decompression of tension pneumothorax is the 5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line (5th ICS AAL), NOT the 2nd ICS midclavicular line. The 5th ICS AAL has thinner chest wall (mean 3.5 cm vs 4.8 cm), higher success rate with a standard 5 cm angiocath (85% vs 50%), and fewer major vessel injuries. In obese patients, an 8 cm angiocath may be needed for the 2nd ICS approach.

For open pneumothorax (sucking chest wound), apply a three-sided occlusive dressing (taped on three sides, with one side left untaped). This creates a 'flutter valve' effect — air can escape on expiration but cannot enter on inspiration. Do NOT use a fully occlusive dressing (taped on all four sides) as this can convert an open pneumothorax to a tension pneumothorax. The definitive treatment is chest tube insertion (28-32 Fr) placed away from the wound, followed by surgical wound closure.

In the ventilated patient with any pneumothorax, even 'occult' (visible only on CT), a chest tube should be placed. Positive pressure ventilation (especially with PEEP) can rapidly convert a small pneumothorax into a life-threatening tension pneumothorax. The threshold for chest tube insertion should be lower in ventilated patients.

Autologous blood patch for persistent air leak: withdraw 50-100 mL of the patient's peripheral blood from a vein, inject it into the chest tube (via the drainage port after cleaning with alcohol), clamp the tube for 1 hour, then unclamp to underwater seal. The blood forms a 'patch' over the air leak site. Success rates: 70-85% after one patch, 85-90% after two. No increased risk of empyema. This is a particularly useful technique in resource-limited settings where VATS is unavailable.

In the Kenyan context where TB-related pneumothorax is common: these patients often have a persistent air leak due to endobronchial TB and poor lung compliance. Chest tube drainage may be required for 2-3 weeks or more. The tube should be connected to an underwater seal and low suction (10 cmH2O). Anti-TB therapy should be started if the patient is not already on treatment. Pleurodesis (talc or blood patch) is often needed. VATS pleurectomy may be challenging due to dense adhesions from chronic TB.

Recurrence prevention: after the first spontaneous pneumothorax, advise the patient to stop smoking (reduces recurrence risk by 50%). After the second ipsilateral pneumothorax, surgical pleurodesis (VATS with bullectomy and mechanical pleurodesis or pleurectomy) is recommended. Patients who are pilots, divers, or in occupations with sudden pressure changes should have definitive treatment after the FIRST episode.

operativeNuances.nuance: For a persistent air leak (>5 days) or failure of lung re-expansion, options include: (1) suction to -20 cmH2O to enhance pleural apposition, (2) blood patch (autologous blood 50-100 mL injected into the chest tube), (3) bedside pleurodesis (talc slurry or doxycycline via chest tube), or (4) VATS with staple bullectomy and mechanical pleurodesis. In the Kenyan context, the blood patch is the most accessible: withdraw 50-100 mL of the patient's peripheral blood and inject it through the chest tube, followed by clamping for 1 hour. Success rates of 70-85% are reported.

VATS technique for persistent pneumothorax: position the patient in lateral decubitus with single-lung ventilation. Use three ports: camera port in 7th ICS mid-axillary line, working ports in 5th ICS anterior axillary line and 6th ICS posterior axillary line. Inspect the entire lung surface for blebs, bullae, and air leaks. For bleb resection: grasp the bleb with atraumatic forceps, lift it, and fire an endoscopic stapler (3.5 mm or 4.8 mm load) across the base of the bleb. Perform mechanical pleurodesis by abrading the parietal pleura using a sterile gauze sponge or dedicated pleural abrader until punctate bleeding points appear. Alternatively, perform an apical pleurectomy by developing a plane between the parietal pleura and endothoracic fascia using blunt dissection and excising the pleura. Insert an apical chest drain under vision, re-expand the lung, and confirm no residual air leak.

Bedside chemical pleurodesis: for patients who are not candidates for VATS or in settings where VATS is unavailable. (1) Talc slurry: mix 4-5 g of sterile talc (sterilized by dry heat at 160°C for 2h if only industrial talc is available) with 50 mL of normal saline. Inject through chest tube, clamp for 2h, then unclamp and put to suction. (2) Doxycycline: 500 mg in 50 mL saline, same technique. Doxycycline causes intense chest pain — pre-medicate with IV morphine 5-10 mg and IV lidocaine 100 mg. (3) Povidone-iodine: 100 mL of 10% povidone-iodine solution. Success rates: talc 80-90%, doxycycline 60-70%, povidone-iodine 70-80%.

Chest tube removal: criteria — lung fully expanded on CXR, no air leak for 24h (observed as no bubbling on underwater seal with cough and deep breath), output <100-200 mL/day. Remove the tube during expiration (to prevent air entry) or at end-inspiration with Valsalva after applying a purse-string suture. Dress the wound with an occlusive dressing. Obtain a post-removal CXR to rule out recurrent pneumothorax. If the patient is on positive pressure ventilation, the chest tube should be placed to water seal (not suction) for 24h before removal, and removed with the ventilator held at end-expiration.

Management of bilateral pneumothorax: this is a surgical emergency. Both sides should be decompressed simultaneously (or the most symptomatic side first, then the other). If the patient is in extremis, bilateral needle decompression (5th ICS AAL) followed by bilateral chest tube insertion. The priority is to relieve tension on both sides. If resources are limited, one team can manage one side while another team manages the other side. Do NOT insert a chest tube on one side and 'wait to see how the other side does' — bilateral pneumothorax requires bilateral drainage.

In HIV-positive patients with PCP-related pneumothorax: the lung tissue is extremely friable and does not hold sutures well. Chest tube insertion should be done carefully to avoid creating a larger lung laceration. The chest tube may need to stay in place longer (10-21 days) due to slow healing. Persistent air leak is common — use low suction (10 cmH2O) to avoid further lung injury. Consider early referral for surgical pleurodesis (VATS or open) if the leak persists beyond 7-10 days. ART optimization is essential for healing.

Chest tube insertion in the 'triangle of safety': the 5th intercostal space at the anterior axillary line is preferred because it avoids: (1) the diaphragm (which rises to the 5th ICS at end-expiration), (2) the internal mammary artery (which runs 2-3 cm lateral to the sternum), (3) the liver on the right (which extends to the 5th ICS), and (4) the spleen on the left (which extends to the 9th ICS). The trocar should never be used for chest tube insertion in trauma — the risk of lung laceration is unacceptably high. Always use blunt dissection with forceps and a finger sweep.

In the Kenyan context where tube thoracostomy kits may not be standardized, improvisation: (1) if no commercial chest tube, use a 20-24 Fr nasogastric tube or a 14-16 Fr Foley catheter with the balloon inflated (2-3 mL) inside the pleural cavity as a substitute; (2) if no underwater seal bottle, use any sterile bottle or container with water, with the drainage tube submerged 2 cm below the water surface; (3) if no Heimlich valve, use a cut surgical glove finger attached to the chest tube (acting as a one-way flutter valve). These improvisations can be life-saving in remote settings.

prognosis.mortality: Tension pneumothorax: 10-20% if decompressed promptly, near 100% if not; simple pneumothorax: <1%
prognosis.recurrenceSpontaneous: 30-50% for primary spontaneous at 5 years if no pleurodesis
prognosis.recurrenceRate_spontaneous: After first episode: 30-50% without pleurodesis; after chemical pleurodesis: 10-20%; after surgical pleurodesis/blebectomy: 2-5%
prognosis.recoveryTimeline: Chest tube 1-5 days; hospital stay 2-7 days; return to work 2-6 weeks; avoid flying for 6 weeks after full resolution
prognosis.kenyanContext: Spontaneous pneumothorax in young adults with TB sequelae is common; delayed presentation with tension pneumothorax is a major cause of preventable death
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Size of pneumothorax (small vs large vs tension), etiology (spontaneous primary vs secondary vs traumatic), presence of tension physiology, and underlying lung disease
